column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Acrylic (PMMA) Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/plastic/thermoplastic/acrylic-pmma-laser-cleaning

column9: Low power for thermal-sensitive polymer cleaning
Larger spot for gentle surface treatment
Lower frequency to prevent thermal damage
Low energy density to avoid melting
Short pulses for minimal thermal impact
Fast scanning to reduce heat accumulation
Multiple gentle passes for gradual cleaning

Typical range for pulsed Nd:YAG lasers in polymer cleaning to avoid thermal damage; starting at 30W for PMMA surface preparation. Max safe ~100W before melting (PMMA melting point ~160°C). Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics and Adapt Laser Systems for delicate substrates.

Effective range for contaminant removal; slower (500-1500 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (2000-5000 mm/s) for light. Recommended 2000 mm/s balances speed and control for PMMA's low thermal conductivity. Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals.

Nanosecond regime typical for industrial 1064 nm cleaning; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond preferred for minimal HAZ but less common. 20 ns recommended to reduce heat accumulation in PMMA. Source: Laser physics research in Optics & Laser Technology (e.g., studies on polymer ablation thresholds).

Standard for cleaning applications; 20 kHz recommended for PMMA to enable fast processing while minimizing heat buildup (higher rates increase accumulation risk). Trade-off: higher freq speeds cleaning but risks substrate damage. Source: Academic literature from Journal of Applied Physics on pulsed laser interactions with polymers.

Fluence below PMMA damage threshold (~1.5-2 J/cm² at 1064 nm for ns pulses) for safe contaminant removal; 0.5 J/cm² optimal to ablate surface layers without substrate melting. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Applied Surface Science (e.g., PMMA IR laser interactions).

Smaller spots (50-200 μm) for fine detail cleaning, larger (200-500 μm) for broad areas; 200 μm recommended for uniform PMMA surface prep considering beam reflectivity. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning systems.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy; 2 passes recommended for complete PMMA cleaning without excessive exposure. Source: Industry case studies from laser cleaning applications in plastics processing (e.g., SPIE proceedings).

Ensures uniform coverage; 70% recommended for even energy distribution on PMMA to prevent uneven heating or missed spots. Source: Process optimization studies in Laser Cleaning Handbook and academic papers on scan strategies.
